title: Parametrized Variational Quantum Tomography
authors: V. A. Penas, M. Losada, D. Tielas, F. Holik
abstract: Quantum state tomography provides a fundamental framework for reconstructing quantum states. When the measurement data are not informationally complete, the observed statistics admit multiple compatible density matrices, making the reconstruction problem inherently underdetermined and calling for the selection of a meaningful estimator. Two well-established approaches to address this ambiguity are Maximum Entropy (MaxEnt) and Variational Quantum Tomography (VQT). A variant of VQT, named VQT$_\infty$, has been introduced to reproduce MaxEnt-like solutions. In this work, we generalize this approach by introducing a parametrized cost function that interpolates between the 1-norm and the infinity norm, thereby unifying VQT and VQT$_\infty$ within a single framework. By tuning the associated hyperparameters, the proposed method enables controlled exploration of the set of compatible density matrices. We show that this interplay yields reconstructed states with higher fidelity to the MaxEnt solution than those obtained via VQT$_\infty$ while preserving computational tractability.
summary: The paper introduces Parametrized Variational Quantum Tomography (PVQT), a method that uses a tunable cost function to bridge the gap between efficient but biased VQT and computationally expensive Maximum Entropy methods. By interpolating between 1-norm and infinity-norm penalties, the authors demonstrate that one can achieve higher-fidelity state reconstruction in underdetermined measurement scenarios.
main_problem: Addressing the ambiguity and intrinsic bias in quantum state reconstruction when measurement data are incomplete (underdetermined).
main_result: The proposed Parametrized Variational Quantum Tomography (PVQT) achieves higher fidelity to the Maximum Entropy solution and higher von Neumann entropy than standard VQT or VQT_infinity by tuning hyperparameters.
method: A new cost function that interpolates between the 1-norm and the infinity norm of unmeasured probabilities, formulated as a linear semidefinite program (SDP).
model_or_system: The study focuses on n-qubit quantum systems (tested up to 5 qubits) using subsets of a Symmetric Informationally Complete POVM (SIC-POVM).
key_observables: Fidelity, trace distance, von Neumann entropy, and Kullback-Leibler (KL) divergence of unmeasured probabilities.
important_parameters: Hyperparameters alpha and beta that control the interpolation between VQT and VQT_infinity; number of measured observables K.
main_assumptions: The method assumes the reconstruction problem is underdetermined due to incomplete POVM measurements; testing is performed on Haar-random pure and mixed states.
figures_summary: Figures show trace distance and fidelity comparisons between MaxEnt, VQT, and VQT_infinity; histograms of fidelity distributions; and performance scaling for 3, 4, and 5-qubit systems under noise.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of incomplete tomography, defines the mathematical framework for VQT and VQT_infinity, proposes the PVQT interpolation method, presents numerical results comparing fidelity and entropy across different qubit counts, and discusses limitations regarding hyperparameter selection.
why_interesting: This is highly relevant for quantum information researchers as it provides a more efficient, scalable alternative to Maximum Entropy tomography, offering a way to control the bias in state reconstruction for noisy, incomplete experimental data.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: Average trace distance between target and reconstructed states for 500 random three-qubit pure states as a function of the number of measured observables, for MaxEnt and VQT∞. The cutoff is set to 32 observables while quorum is 64.

FIG. 2: Average fidelity between target and reconstructed states for 500 random three-qubit pure states as a function of the number of measured observables, for MaxEnt and VQT∞. The cutoff is set to 32 as in Fig. 1.

FIG. 3: (a) Histograms of fidelities between VQT∞and MaxEnt reconstructed states, over 500 random three-qubit pure states. (b) Average fidelity between VQT∞and MaxEnt reconstructed states. Error bars represent quartiles. Note that mean (average) and median are close to each other but they are not equal.

FIG. 4: Average fidelity between target and reconstructed states for 500 random three-qubit pure states as a function of the number of measured observables, for MaxEnt, VQT∞, and PVQT with β = 0.01 and β = 0.003.

FIG. 5: (a) Histograms of fidelities between PVQT with β = 0.01 and MaxEnt reconstructed states, over 500 random three-qubit pure states. (b) Average fidelity between PVQT with β = 0.01 and MaxEnt reconstructed states. Error bars represent quartiles. Note that mean (average) and median are close to each other but they are not equal.
FIG. 6: Average fidelity between different VQTs and MaxEnt reconstructed states for 500 random three-qubit pure states as a function of the number of measured observables.

FIG. 7: Average Kullback-Leibler divergence between the uniform distribution and the unmeasured probability vector ⃗u of different VQTs and MaxEnt reconstructed states, for 500 random three-qubit pure states, as a function of the number of measured observables.

FIG. 9: Average fidelity between different VQTs and MaxEnt reconstructed states for 500 random three-qubit pure states as a function of the number of measured ob- servables, with 5% uniform noise added to the measure- ment outcomes.
FIG. 8: Average Von Neumann entropy for different VQTs and MaxEnt reconstructed states for 500 random three-qubit pure states as a function of the number of measured observables.

FIG. 10: Average fidelity between different VQTs and MaxEnt reconstructed states for 500 random three-qubit rank-2 states as a function of the number of measured observables, with 5% uniform noise added to the mea- surement outcomes. Cutoff around 45 observables.
